original_abstract: The catalytic conversion of carbon dioxide into methanol represents a critical pathway for the utilization of captured emissions, yet conventional copper-based catalysts typically require high operating pressures to achieve significant yields. This study explores the performance of size-selected $\text{Cu}_4$ clusters supported on $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ thin films for the hydrogenation of $\text{CO}_2$ under near-atmospheric conditions. Using in situ grazing-incidence X-ray absorption spectroscopy, we characterized the oxidation state of the clusters during the reaction, revealing that the $\text{Cu}_4$ species are highly active in their reduced state at low $\text{CO}_2$ partial pressures. Experimental results demonstrate that these subnanometer clusters exhibit exceptional turn-over rates, surpassing those of recently reported low-pressure catalysts. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations support these observations, identifying a lower activation barrier for the conversion of $\text{CO}_2$ to $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ on $\text{Cu}_4$ compared to bulk copper surfaces or larger nanoparticles. This enhanced activity is attributed to the unique coordination environment and strong adsorption properties of the small clusters. Our findings suggest that atom-precisely engineered copper clusters offer a highly efficient route for recycling $\text{CO}_2$ into valuable chemical feedstocks at reduced energy costs.
humanized_abstract: The catalytic conversion of carbon dioxide into methanol is one of the strategies for utilizing the captured CO2. However, the widely used copper catalysts need to operate at high pressure to achieve a substantial amount of product. Here, we investigated the performance of size-selected Cu4 clusters supported on Al2O3 thin films for the CO2 hydrogenation under near-atmospheric pressure. In situ grazing-incidence X-ray absorption spectroscopy (GI-XAS) was employed to investigate the oxidation state of the clusters under reaction conditions. It was found that the Cu4 clusters were highly active in their reduced state at low CO2 partial pressure. The subnanometer Cu4 clusters exhibited the highest turn-over rates (TORs) compared to other low-pressure catalysts. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations further supported the high activity of Cu4 clusters toward CO2 hydrogenation by comparing the activation barriers for the transformation of CO2 into CH3OH on Cu4 clusters, as well as on bulk copper surfaces and larger copper nanoparticles. The DFT results indicated that Cu4 clusters possess a lower activation barrier than that on the copper surfaces and larger nanoparticles. This is attributed to the specific coordination sites on Cu4 clusters and the strong adsorption of reactants on the clusters. Overall, our studies reveal that atom-precisely engineered copper clusters can be an efficient catalyst for utilizing CO2 into valuable chemicals at a lower energy input.
